## [00:00 - 03:04]

G'day, welcome back to the final module. You've made it of AdWords Fundamentals, Module 10 and in this module I am going to cover four slightly more advanced topics. So this is going to be a slightly longer module. I'm not going to go in super super detail into these but just to introduce you to some of these more advanced topics that I think are going to be the important ones to get you from beginner to intermediate so that you can really start to get some decent results from your AdWords accounts to make sure you don't lose your shirt and so you can start running AdWords accounts for clients which I'm going to assume is one of your goals if you're going through this or at least get your AdWords account and running better for your business if that's the reason that you're doing it. So the first one of our four is bidding. So this is really part of the game inside of AdWords, the fact that you can bid differently for every single keyword in your account if you want to. Again I'm going to suggest that at the beginning just use manual bidding. You don't need any of Google's fancy automation. Also better for you to understand how things work and to understand exactly what you can and can't do and for you to retain control because quite frankly the Google machine as I've mentioned this before but as much as they're trying to build robots and automation and artificial intelligence to manage all of your bids for you and they're not quite there yet. Typically when we use those results go down. So we will continue to test those and I'll let you know when the machine is unbeatable. I reckon that'll probably happen sometime in 2018, possibly 2019, but probably sometime in 2018 it'll be virtually impossible to beat the machine but for now yeah just be careful. Some of these bidding strategies that you can use I'm going to give you three. Just as a way to dip your toe into this world of automated bidding and the first is Enhanced CPC. You'll find this as an option. I mean if you can't find it inside your AdWords account just do a search for AdWords Help Enhanced CPC and you'll find the page in the AdWords Help Center that describes how to apply this in your account but this essentially gives Google a little bit of control. So it kind of respects all of the various settings that you have in your campaigns and it looks at what you're prepared to bid. Let's say it's $2 a click and then it allows a little bit of wiggle room above that and below that and it's going to choose to change that bid based on how likely it believes that particular person

## [03:04 - 06:06]

viewing your ad right now how likely they are to convert or not because Google knows not only how all of us are using Google AdWords and it can see obviously inside of every single AdWords account and sees all of the keywords that you're bidding on as well as all of your competitors and how you're all doing relative to one another but it also has enormous amounts of data on the other side. It knows an awful lot about that person that is searching for your products and services right now. It knows where they are. It knows the context of that search pretty well. It knows if this person is normally in South Africa or whether they're just there on holiday at the moment. It knows if they're on a mobile device or a desktop. It knows what the weather is in that location. Obviously it knows the time of day. Google have even tested if the phase of the moon influenced how likely it was that people were going to convert. It didn't but hey they tested it so you can guarantee that they've tested an awful lot of different things. In fact Google uses in every single auction in those milliseconds that it takes when you do a search on Google to when you see that page of results with the ads at the top. Google has considered 70 million. That's a 7 with a ton of zeros. 70 million data points in order to choose which ads it showed and who's going to spend how much. And this is what I'm talking about you know and potentially in the very near future not being able to beat the machine at making those kinds of decisions because Google just doesn't give us that level of data. So the next level up is target CPA and that's where we start to hand the reins over to Google and say my target is 50 bucks. See if you can get me leads for 50 bucks please Google. And it will then choose when to show your ad or not and how much to bid and a whole lot of other stuff that we don't need to worry about where it's just going to aim to get you leads or sales at that target level that you've told it. Now sometimes again this goes completely off the rails and you can set a target bid of 50 bucks and Google comes back with 130 dollar sales all day long. The machine is learning though and it's learning every minute it's getting smarter and that's why my prediction is we will not be able to beat it by the end of 2018. We'll see if that prediction comes right or not. But once you're more familiar with Google AdWords and you've been running your own accounts manually for a little bit once you've got a decent level of conversions and I'm going to suggest that you don't even think about target CPA until you're getting 100 conversions a month. Once you're getting more than 100 conversions and again conversions for you could be a lead, it could be somebody opting in for your video, it could be somebody buying a hundred dollar item, whatever it is for you if you're getting more than about

## [06:06 - 09:08]

100 a month, 500 a month is a wonderful place to be because the machine has more data then you can hand the reins over to Google and say go on then show me what you can do and sometimes it will do amazing things and other times not so good. And the last one on here is target ROAS or ROAS and that stands for return on ad spend. Now you're probably only going to worry about this if you are a retailer, if you're an e-com store and you're very, very concerned about your ROI so you need to make at least say four to one on every dollar that you spend. So every hundred dollars you tip into the machine you need to get at least $400 back out in revenue. Most of our shopping clients run at around seven or eight to one, it depends a little bit on the store. We've got some that are very, very profitable at five and others that are running at 20 to one. It really just depends on what you're selling. But you can then have this, it's similar to the target CPA but it's taking into account obviously the different revenues for the different sales. I think I used an example earlier in the course you might have one sale that's $500 and one sale that's $50. If you're selling lots of different items then ROAS is a better KPI, a better measure of success than just CPA which is just did I sell a thing? I don't really care which thing, just did I sell a thing? Return on ad spend says yep I sold something and what was the revenue from that? What was my return on that? And again just like with target CPA you can set a target in there and say go on then Google, I set my target at seven to one, let's see what you can do. Get me at least $7 back for each dollar and if you can do that then I'll be happy to up my budgets. So they are of all of the different automated bidding strategies inside AdWords. Those are the ones that I would suggest you start with and I would start with enhanced CPC and DiPito in the water there. Now I mentioned negative keywords a little bit in the earlier module on keywords. I just wanted to give a couple more examples and make sure that we've got this really squared away because using negative keywords is one of the best and fastest ways to improve any AdWords account and as I mentioned before negatives stop your ads appearing for certain terms and this was the example I used before when we were talking about TVs. You might have necks such as free or second hand, vintage used, I think I've used the example of parts and servicing, stuff like that. So Google I do not want you to show my ad if the user's search query contains this particular word and let's just look at why this is so important for a minute. Remember that we when we talked a little bit about quality score in the previous module what I didn't explain in the quality score module was that the click-through rate

## [09:08 - 12:13]

of your ad which is simply just the percentage of people that click your ads. So if 20,000 people see the ad and a thousand people click a thousand divided by 20,000 is 5%. That click-through rate is a very very strong signal. Google looks at a lot of things but that is a very strong signal that tells Google are people finding what they were searching for. In other words is this a good ad? If it's a good ad then I should show it more often and I should give this advertiser mate's because this advertiser is good for me. It's good for Google's users, people are finding what they want. That's a good thing. Let's give them some cheaper clicks. If the click-through rate is really bad then the opposite is going to happen. Your quality scores are going to go down and over time you're going to spend more and more for those clicks. Now I can't say what a good or bad number is because it doesn't really matter what the number is. What matters is what is your click-through rate compared to your competitors. If we want to be really technical about this it's compared to your competitors for the same keyword on the same position on the page because Google knows if you show an ad at the top of the page you're going to have a much much higher click-through rate than if your ad is down the bottom of the page. So Google's going to take that into account but if you're showing an ad in position two compared to your competitor in position two what are your relative click-through rates for each keyword over time because if your click-through rates are much much better then that's when Google's going to reward you. It's going to give you higher quality scores and it's going to give you cheaper clicks. Why NEGS are so important is imagine the situation where we've shown an ad 20,000 times but actually half of those were junk search queries. It's stuff that we really shouldn't be showing ads for at all and if we can add the right negative keywords and get rid of half of these impressions we'd be left with a 10,000 impressions. Now I'm going to make the assumptions that sorry the assumption here that that most of those thousand clicks we got before we would still get most of those. I'm going to make the assumption that 100 of those clicks were idiot clicks. People searching for stuff that we don't have but they still clicked on our ad. I'm going to assume therefore that once we've got rid of all those junk search queries we still get roughly the same number of clicks. It's gone down a little bit but we're still getting a lot more clicks. So now 900 divided by 10,000 that gets us a click-through rate of 9% almost double and Google is going to like that. If you click-through rate doubles and all we've done is just use a few good negative keywords and sends a really strong signal to Google to say, yep, smart advertiser, charge them less. So where do you find negative keywords? As I mentioned in an earlier module your search query report is probably the first place that

you want to look. That's the main place that you're going to find them.
